Travel for LiFE highlights cooperation between government, tourists, business and communities, this reflects:
questions.passage:
"Travel for LIFE" is a global movement towards sustainable tourism. This movement is inspired by mission LIFE (Lifestyle for Environment). It seeks to inspire both tourist and tourism business to embrace concept of pro-environmental behaviour, circular economy, and shared economy. The ultimate goal of this initiative is to create a brighter future. In the pursuit of the 2030 Global Agenda, nations are making significant strides toward a world devoid of poverty, gender inequality and economic inequality. Mission LiFE embodies the spirit of the pro-planet people (P3) model and function on the basic principles of "Lifestyle of the planet, for the planet and by the planet."

Travel for LIFE (TFL) call for a transition from "use and dispose" economy to circular economy characterized by mindful utilization of resources instead of mindless destructive consumption of resources. TFL programme will nudge tourists and tourism business to practice lifestyle that is synchronous with nature and does not harm it. It aims to encourage large scale behavioural changes and mainstream sustainability into tourism sector. The TFL programme identifies eight themes to address various aspects of sustainability. These themes are save energy, save water, say no to single use plastic, reduce waste, empower local business and communities, respect local culture and heritage, consume local food, and conserve natural heritage.

questions.questionText: The service dimension in 'Heal in India' positioning is best reflected by:
options: Cost advantage of one tenth of western treatment charges
Patient facilitation centers and multilingual staff
questions.passage:
The strategic positioning of 'Heal in India' as a global medical and health tourism brand requires a multi-dimensional approach. India offers diverse climatic conditions, landscapes and biodiversity that underpin wellness Tourism. Advanced medical infrastructure, highly skilled professionals and internationally accredited facilities are appealing the international market.

The medical treatments and surgeries are available at a fraction of costs as compared to western countries. Tourism infrastructure such as international airports and streamlined visa system with soft infrastructure including digital health records, teleconsultation and hybrid follow-up mechanisms is within reach of the customers. Patients also appreciate holistic care through internationally trained doctors, JCI/NABH accreditation, multilingual staff which are integrated with wellness packages.

India is framed as a comprehensive healthcare hub covering evidence-based medicine with traditional AYUSH system. Collectively these strategies establish affordability, cultural integration, wellness depth while maintaining safety accreditation and International standards.

The medical treatments and surgeries are available at a fraction of costs as compared to western countries. Tourism infrastructure such as international airports and streamlined visa system with soft infrastructure including digital health records, teleconsultation and hybrid follow-up mechanisms is within reach of the customers. Patients also appreciate holistic care through internationally trained doctors, JCI/NABH accreditation, multilingual staff which are integrated with wellness packages.

India is framed as a comprehensive healthcare hub covering evidence-based medicine with traditional AYUSH system. Collectively these strategies establish affordability, cultural integration, wellness depth while maintaining safety accreditation and International standards.

questions.questionText: Which element does NOT fall under accessibility in "Heal in India" positioning?
options: Integration of AYUSH therapies with clinical treatments
questions.passage:
The strategic positioning of 'Heal in India' as a global medical and health tourism brand requires a multi-dimensional approach. India offers diverse climatic conditions, landscapes and biodiversity that underpin wellness Tourism. Advanced medical infrastructure, highly skilled professionals and internationally accredited facilities are appealing the international market.

The medical treatments and surgeries are available at a fraction of costs as compared to western countries. Tourism infrastructure such as international airports and streamlined visa system with soft infrastructure including digital health records, teleconsultation and hybrid follow-up mechanisms is within reach of the customers. Patients also appreciate holistic care through internationally trained doctors, JCI/NABH accreditation, multilingual staff which are integrated with wellness packages.

India is framed as a comprehensive healthcare hub covering evidence-based medicine with traditional AYUSH system. Collectively these strategies establish affordability, cultural integration, wellness depth while maintaining safety accreditation and International standards.
